audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Joshua / Yehoshua Manasseh inheritance, Zelophehad's daughters, Canaanite persistence, and Joseph-tribe complaint, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Machir/Gilead, clan allotments, Zelophehad's daughters receiving inheritance, Manasseh boundary/enclave complexity, non-dispossessed Canaanites, forced labor, Joseph's complaint, forest-clearing, and iron-chariot rhetoric.

verses.restored: Lot came for tribe of Manasseh, for he was firstborn of Joseph; for Machir firstborn of Manasseh, father of Gilead, because he was a man of war, Gilead and Bashan were his.

There was also for remaining sons of Manasseh according to their clans: sons of Abiezer, Helek, Asriel, Shechem, Hepher, and Shemida; these were male children of Manasseh son of Joseph according to their clans.
But Zelophehad son of Hepher son of Gilead son of Machir son of Manasseh had no sons, only daughters; these were names of his daughters: Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.

They drew near before Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and leaders, saying, YHWH commanded Moses to give us inheritance among our brothers. So according to mouth of YHWH he gave them inheritance among brothers of their father.
Ten portions fell to Manasseh, apart from land of Gilead and Bashan across Jordan,
because daughters of Manasseh received inheritance among his sons, and land of Gilead belonged to remaining sons of Manasseh.
Border of Manasseh was from Asher to Michmethath, which is before Shechem; border went rightward to inhabitants of En-tappuah.
Land of Tappuah belonged to Manasseh, but Tappuah on border of Manasseh belonged to sons of Ephraim.
Border went down to wadi Kanah, south of wadi; these cities belonged to Ephraim among cities of Manasseh. Border of Manasseh was north of wadi, and its end was sea.
Southward it was Ephraim's and northward Manasseh's, and sea was his border; they met Asher on north and Issachar on east.

In Issachar and Asher, Manasseh had Beth-shean and its towns, Ibleam and its towns, inhabitants of Dor and its towns, inhabitants of En-dor and its towns, inhabitants of Taanach and its towns, inhabitants of Megiddo and its towns, three heights.
Sons of Manasseh were not able to dispossess these cities, and Canaanite was determined to dwell in this land.
It was, when children of Israel grew strong, that they put Canaanite to forced labor, but they did not surely dispossess him.
Children of Joseph spoke to Joshua, saying, Why have you given me inheritance of one lot and one portion, though I am a numerous people, since until now YHWH has blessed me?
Joshua said to them, If you are a numerous people, go up for yourself to forest and clear for yourself there in land of Perizzite and Rephaim, since hill country of Ephraim is too narrow for you.

Children of Joseph said, Hill country will not be found enough for us, and iron chariots are with all Canaanites dwelling in land of valley, those in Beth-shean and its towns and those in valley of Jezreel.
Joshua spoke to house of Joseph, to Ephraim and Manasseh, saying, You are a numerous people and great power is yours; you shall not have one lot only.

But hill country shall be yours; though forest it is, you shall clear it, and its outer limits shall be yours, for you shall dispossess Canaanite, though iron chariots are his and though he is strong.

translation_notes: The daughters of Zelophehad—Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah—successfully invoke YHWH's command through Moses and receive inheritance among their father's brothers.
Their appearance continues the gender-inheritance correction from Numbers 27 and 36 and should be read as women's legal agency within a still-patriarchal land system.
Manasseh's allotment includes overlapping and enclave-like arrangements with Ephraim, Issachar, and Asher, showing that tribal geography is not a neat modern map.
The text admits Canaanites remained in several cities and were determined to dwell there, contradicting total-erasure rhetoric.
Israel's later strength produces forced labor rather than equal coexistence. This is coercive domination, not justice.
Joshua's forest-clearing answer links inheritance to labor, but the command still ends with dispossessing strong Canaanite communities with iron chariots.
The chapter's land claim must not be used for modern displacement, forced labor, or denial of surviving communities' rights.
safety_note: Do not use Joshua 17 to justify displacement, forced labor, ethnic domination, or treating unresolved land pressure as permission to remove current inhabitants. Center the daughters of Zelophehad as legal agents, the text's admission of mixed populations, and the ethical need for inheritance without exploitation.
